summary: Joel Spolsky explains a bug in Excel 2007 where multiplying 77.1 by 850 displays 100,000 instead of 65,535, drawing on his experience as a former Excel team member. He clarifies that the bug is purely in the display formatting code, not in the actual calculation engine — downstream calculations using the value produce correct results. The root cause involves IEEE 754 floating point representation, where 0.1 has no exact binary representation, causing the product to be extremely close to but not exactly 65,535, which triggers a rare formatting bug affecting only 12 out of all possible floating point numbers. Spolsky argues the bug's real-world impact is negligible but uses it as a springboard to discuss floating point arithmetic, testing limitations, and takes a parting shot at the current Excel team's competence.
key_points: The Excel bug is purely a display formatting issue — the underlying binary value is correct and produces accurate results in further calculations and charts
IEEE 754 floating point cannot exactly represent 0.1 in binary (it's a repeating binary fraction), so 77.1*850 produces a value extremely close to but not exactly 65,535
The bug only affects 12 out of 18,446,744,073,709,551,616 possible floating point numbers, making it nearly impossible to catch with black-box testing
Automated VBA-based testing wouldn't catch this because VBA receives the correct binary value before display formatting occurs
Arbitrary-precision arithmetic could solve floating point issues but has historically been too slow for spreadsheet use, though modern CPUs might handle it
money_quotes: 0.1 has no exact representation in binary… it's a repeating binary number. It's sort of like how 1/3 has no representation in decimal.
If you tried to do 3*1/3, and you didn't have time to write 3's forever, the result you would get would be 0.99999999, not 1, and people would get angry with you for being wrong.
The chance that you would see this in real life calculations is microscopic. Better worry about getting hit by a meteorite.
With a bug like this that only happens on 12 out of 18446744073709551616 possible floating point binary numbers, it's unlikely that any set of black-box tests would cover this case.
The Microsoft Excel support team has spent the last 20 years defending IEEE 754, and it's not surprising that they've started to believe in it.

Do you really want the bright sparks who work there now, and manage to break lots of perfectly good working code — rewriting the core calculating engine in Excel? Better keep them busy adding and removing dancing paper clips all day long.
key_insight: The Excel 2007 display bug illustrates how IEEE 754 floating point's inherent imprecision can interact with formatting code to produce visually alarming but computationally harmless errors that are nearly impossible to catch through testing.
